title: Strings & f-strings
subtitle: Learn to play with text like a word wizard — and meet the magic of f-strings.
goal: Make, join, slice, and change text, then use f-strings to mix words and variables together cleanly.

blocks.heading: What is a string? 📝
blocks.html:
A string is just text — letters, spaces, emojis, anything you can type. We wrap text in quotes so Python knows it's a string.

You can use single quotes 'like this' or double quotes "like this". They both work the same way — pick whichever you like!

blocks.caption: Single quotes and double quotes both make strings
blocks.code:
print('Hello with single quotes')
print("Hello with double quotes")
blocks.explain: Both lines are strings. Single and double quotes do the same job — just keep them matched at the start and end.

blocks.html: Handy trick: if your text has an apostrophe like It's fun, use double quotes around it: "It's fun". That way the apostrophe doesn't confuse Python.

blocks.heading: Joining and repeating text ➕✖️
blocks.html: You can glue strings together with + (we call this concatenation), and repeat a string with *. Watch this:

blocks.caption: Glue with + and repeat with *
blocks.code:
first = "Lego"
second = "Master"
print(first + second)
print(first + " " + second)
print("ha" * 5)
print("-" * 20)
blocks.explain: + joins strings end to end (it does NOT add a space — you add that yourself with " "). * repeats a string. "ha" * 5 gives you a big laugh, and "-" * 20 makes a quick line.

blocks.html: Watch out! + only glues string to string. You can't do "Age: " + 9 — Python will complain. We'll fix that with f-strings in a moment! 🪄

blocks.heading: How long? Counting and peeking inside 🔢
blocks.html:
len() tells you how many characters are in a string. And every character has a position number called an index, starting at 0.

So in "cat": c is index 0, a is index 1, t is index 2. You can also count from the end with negative numbers: -1 is the last character.

blocks.caption: len(), indexing, and slicing
blocks.explain: len(word) is 6 letters. word[0] is the first letter 'P'. word[-1] is the last letter 'n'. word[0:3] is a SLICE — it grabs letters 0, 1, 2 ('Pyt') but stops just before 3.

blocks.question: In the string "Hello", what does Hello[0] give you?
blocks.explain: Indexing starts at 0, so position 0 is the very first character — the letter H.

blocks.heading: String superpowers: handy methods 🦸
blocks.html:
Strings come with built-in methods — little tools you call with a dot. Here are some favorites:

  • .upper() → SHOUTING
  • .lower() → quiet
  • .title() → Capitalizes Each Word
  • .strip() → removes extra spaces at the ends
  • .replace(a, b) → swaps one bit of text for another
  • .count(x) → counts how many times x appears

blocks.caption: Trying out string methods
blocks.code:
name = "  charlie brown  "
print(name.strip())
print(name.strip().title())
print("WHISPER".lower())
print("banana".count("a"))
print("I like cats".replace("cats", "dogs"))
blocks.explain: Methods don't change the original string — they hand you back a NEW one. Notice you can chain them: .strip().title() cleans the spaces AND capitalizes each word.

blocks.question: What does "hello".upper() give you?
blocks.explain: .upper() turns every letter into a capital, so you get HELLO.

blocks.heading: The star of the show: f-strings 🌟
blocks.html:
Mixing words and variables with + gets messy fast — extra quotes, missing spaces, and errors when you try to glue numbers.

f-strings fix all of that. Put the letter f right before the opening quote, then drop any variable inside curly braces { }. Python fills in the values for you!

blocks.caption: The messy old way vs the clean f-string way
blocks.code:
name = "Maya"
age = 9

# Old way with + (clunky, and you must turn age into a string)
print("Hi " + name + ", you are " + str(age))

# New way with an f-string (so clean!)
print(f"Hi {name}, you are {age}")
blocks.explain: With +, you needed str(age) to glue a number, plus careful spaces and quotes. The f-string just drops {name} and {age} right where you want them. Same result, way easier to read.

blocks.caption: f-strings can do little calculations too
blocks.code:
name = "Alex"
apples = 3
friends = 2
print(f"We have {apples} apples and {friends} friends.")
print(f"That is {apples + friends} things in total!")
print(f"In CAPS: {name.upper()}")
blocks.explain: Inside the curly braces you can put math like apples + friends, or even call methods like name.upper(). Python figures it out and slots the answer right in.

blocks.question: If name = "Sam", what does f"Hi {name}!" print?
blocks.explain: The f before the quote tells Python to replace {name} with its value, Sam. So you get: Hi Sam!

blocks.title: Make a friendly greeting with an f-string
blocks.instructions: Fill in the name and age, then use an f-string to print a greeting. Press Check when it prints a sentence that includes the name.
blocks.starter:
name = "___"
age = 10
print(f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old!")
blocks.solution:
name = "Zoe"
age = 10
print(f"Hello {name}, you are {age} years old!")
blocks.passMsg: Nice! You just used an f-string like a pro. 🌟
blocks.failMsg: Keep the f-string (the f before the quotes) and make sure you print a name and age inside the { } braces.

blocks.title: Shout your name
blocks.instructions: Make a variable with your name, then print it in ALL CAPS using .upper(). Try doing it inside an f-string!
blocks.starter:
name = "___"
print(f"My name in caps is {name.upper()}")
blocks.solution:
name = "riley"
print(f"My name in caps is {name.upper()}")
blocks.failMsg: Use .upper() on your name. You can call it right inside the { } of the f-string.

challenge.title: Build a superhero name card
challenge.instructions: Create variables for a hero name, a power, and a power level (a number). Then use f-strings to print a little card: a top border, a line introducing the hero, a line about their power, and a bottom border. Make it cool!
challenge.starter:
hero = "___"
power = "___"
level = 100
print("=" * 25)
# print a line with the hero name using an f-string
# print a line with the power and level using an f-string
print("=" * 25)
challenge.solution:
hero = "Captain Comet"
power = "super speed"
level = 100
print("=" * 25)
print(f"Hero: {hero}")
print(f"Power: {power} (level {level})")
print("=" * 25)
challenge.hint: Use "=" * 25 for the borders, and f-strings like f"Hero: {hero}" for the middle lines. You need at least four print() lines.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Epic card! You've mastered strings and f-strings — Day 3 complete, word wizard!
